summary: External-review improvement cycle for CC-0092 addressing sourcery-ai[bot] feedback on potential nil-pointer dereference in pushSecretRelevantChangePredicate.Update. The fix (a) replaces `GetDeletionTimestamp().IsZero()` with an explicit `== nil` presence check on the returned *metav1.Time, removing implicit dependency on metav1.Time.IsZero's nil-receiver guard, (b) adds an early-return guard `if e.ObjectOld == nil || e.ObjectNew == nil { return true }` at the start of UpdateFunc as defense in depth, (c) updates the predicate docblock and docs/reference/keystone-reconciler.md to describe the presence flip as `nil` vs non-`nil` with a rationale sentence, and (d) logs the review interaction in .planwerk/reviews/ and extends the guard-shell-commands review pattern with a CC-0092 occurrence. Semantic behavior of the predicate is unchanged (the two forms are equivalent because the apiserver never sets DeletionTimestamp to a non-nil zero-time value). Build, vet, race-enabled unit tests for TestPushSecretPredicate_* all pass.

tests_checklist.item: All TestPushSecretPredicate_* tests still pass under -race after the predicate edit
TestPushSecretPredicate_StatusOnlyUpdateSuppressed continues to exercise the presence-flip path (both old and new DeletionTimestamp nil) through the new `== nil` comparison
TestPushSecretPredicate_DeletionTimestampSetAdmitted continues to exercise the nil → non-nil presence flip through the new comparison
reconcile_secrets.go / reconcile_secrets_test.go / TestIntegration_Finalizer* untouched by this improvement cycle (REQ-005 preserved)

code_quality_checklist.item: Presence check is nil-safe without relying on metav1.Time.IsZero's nil-receiver guard
Docblock on pushSecretRelevantChangePredicate explains why `== nil` is preferred and notes apiserver invariant makes the two forms equivalent
docs/reference/keystone-reconciler.md Watches-table row agrees verbatim with the code's presence-check phrasing
CC-0092 feature ID carried on all new doc comments and the docs row (FEATURE_ID_REQUIRED)

security_checklist.item: No new untrusted inputs introduced; predicate still operates on controller-runtime event objects only
No new logging of secret material

architecture_checklist.item: Fix is minimal and localized to UpdateFunc + its docblock + one docs row + one review-pattern memo entry
No change to Watches() registration, mapper, or integration paths
Semantic equivalence with the pre-fix predicate preserved (same admit/suppress decisions for every event shape)

dry_yagni_checklist.item: No new helpers or abstractions introduced for a one-line condition change
Doc rationale lives inline at the predicate (single source of truth) and is mirrored once in the reference doc

fail_fast_checklist.item: Nil-event early return (`return true`) is a conservative admit-on-malformed-event posture consistent with the existing Create/Delete/Generic always-admit policy

defensive_checklist.item: The defensive ObjectOld/ObjectNew nil guard is accepted under EXTERNAL REVIEW PROTECTION — the sourcery-ai feedback explicitly asked for nil-safety, so the guard is not flagged as CLAUDE.md 'trust framework guarantees' overreach

suggested_improvements: Optional: when a future PR next touches this predicate, consider adding a TestPushSecretPredicate_NilObjectsAdmitted case to lock in the early-return contract the sourcery-ai review asked for. It is not required here (controller-runtime's UpdateEvent contract guarantees non-nil objects, so the guard is defense-in-depth only), but the test would document the intent for future readers. Not a blocker — adding a test for a scenario the framework guarantees can't happen is the kind of 'tests that only verify language semantics' that the codebase's review patterns push back on, so this remains an optional nicety rather than a finding.
next_steps: Mark .planwerk/progress/CC-0092-*.json phase back to `review_complete` / `awaiting_external_review` once this review is persisted and the improvement cycle is closed.
